title: Regina Hall Interview on Scary Movie 6 and Kevin Hart Roast

blocks.content: https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/b9e/a39/f819ec391c3ee711faa9e7ae54b9755df7-Regina-Hall-Simone-Niamani-Thompson.rvertical.w570.jpg
This article was featured in New York’s One Great Story newsletter. Sign up here.

Regina Hall has stealthily become one of the defining ensemble actors of Black studio films. She has played both the straight woman and the comic relief in films like The Best Man and Girls Trip. For years, critics treated this as charm rather than craft. Not until she transitioned to prestige turns in Support the Girls, Honk for Jesus. Save Your Soul., Black Monday, and the Oscar-winning One Battle After Another were the very traits that gave her such command of humor properly recognized. “People saw me as a broad comedic actress,” she says, “and so I needed to convince them that you could just do a grounded version of stuff.” Later this month, she reprises her role as the vulgar and lovable Brenda Meeks in the Wayans family’s Scary Movie franchise. “It’s special when you are making a film that you know your audience is going to resonate with,” Hall says. “If you’re making a movie for a demographic that doesn’t get to see themselves as much as they would hope, that’s a gift.”

You went to graduate school for journalism at NYU before you pivoted to acting. Why had you considered journalism?I’m from Washington, D.C., and news was important. At that time — well, we still have the Post, but, you know — it felt very stable. I was like, Well, we’re never gonna not have the newspapers and information. Journalism is so connected to the integrity of democracy, so I never thought of it as unstable.

Does it bother you when journalists ask the same questions at press junkets?They have a small framework they have to write, and they need those beats. I know how a sound bite can look. I know how things can sound. So it makes me conscious and thoughtful most of the time when I am speaking.

You got a relatively late start in acting when you were in your late 20s. How did entering an industry where youth is so prioritized affect you?Well, at that time I looked really young, so I still could play very young. But that wasn’t really what I was thinking about. My father had just passed away suddenly. He was an electrician, and my mom was a teacher, so I had felt some financial stability. But then he died, and it was like, Wow, life is not linear. And is so unpredictable. I found, for me, that work gave me relief from the sadness and grief of that time.

Do you consider yourself a funny person?I guess, yeah, but I also think a lot of people are funny; my family’s funny. I think a lot of people aren’t, but then everybody has their own humor. It’s always a nice compliment, though. I didn’t think that much of it until I started hearing from people who were like, “I watched this when I was sick,” or had cancer. It was great to know it’s something that is healing for people.

Most people would agree that your breakout role was Brenda, the irreverent voice of reason in Scary Movie. Did you feel that when you got it? Like, This is the role that’s about to do something for me?I don’t know that I thought in that way. I just was like, I got a job. The Best Man wasn’t out yet and neither was Love & Basketball, so I didn’t even understand what a movie did or didn’t do. I was just very excited to work with the Wayans; I felt like I was going to learn so much because I was going to work with Keenen, and I had never really specifically done comedy. It was my first job that was “run of picture,” where it wasn’t like you only come in for a certain number of days. I thought it would be another great funny movie of theirs, like Don’t Be a Menace to South Central While Drinking Your Juice in the Hood.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/9a2/98e/acd75417bc67ced811bc08bac448b23441-1999-The-Best-Man.rhorizontal.w700.jpg
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/0f5/d7f/f27999d68d65bbfeb6c54b8b686151847d-2000-Scary-Movie.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

How did your audition with Keenen Ivory Wayans go? I’m sure there are like 100 actors trying to make him laugh on any given day.I auditioned a few times before I got to him; I had to pre-read and read again. I had on this handkerchief-top outfit and matching pants with my hair half up — it was not good, probably, but I thought it was cute. For some reason, I was like, Oh, this is Brenda. I talked to one of my brothers and told him I was gonna ad-lib, and it was really exciting. I remember Keenen went, “You’re funny. Where are you from, Regina?” That was the first time I had ever met him.

How did you approach acting in a comedy for the first time?Keenen’s guidance was to be in the moment and have fun. I just read it and would see the space for humor. I don’t know if you can teach it to that degree, but you can certainly encourage it, foster it, and manipulate it, and I think Keenen knew how to do that. He gave me such creative room and support. The biggest thing he said to me and Anna Faris was “There’s no vanity in comedy.” That acknowledgment from someone who really understood comedy and whom I respected so much was incredibly encouraging, and that’s what enables you to risk it. I learned that kind of instinctiveness working with him.

The Wayanses’ film work sits between satire and provocation. How did you figure out that line for yourself over the years?I grew up when that was what comedy was. It was Eddie Murphy, Richard Pryor, Redd Foxx. The Wayanses’ style of broad comedy lets you be a little bit bigger and a little bit more because the world it exists in is already heightened — it’s a world already in extremes. They teach you how to hone your own comedic instinct. We just wanted to make Keenen laugh; he was such a joy to work with because he had an eye on what was too much.

He was also really confident around set. We didn’t have that many Black film directors then — Malcolm D. Lee and Gina Prince-Bythewood were first-time filmmakers, whereas Keenen had been doing In Living Color, and I felt really safe within that.

Do you have any idea of what made Brenda such a breakout character? I’m sure people still come up to you talking about her.They do. It’s so different for different people. Brenda’s pretty selfish, and the worst friend — I mean, she prayed for Cindy’s death. Maybe people resonate with her honesty. She’s fun to play because she gets to be whatever. I just think the world they’ve created allows all of these characters to exist in ways that we can live vicariously through them. There’s the person who wanted to stab a Brenda in the movie theater; we know that person. We all know a Brenda or have been a Brenda. Maybe that’s it.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/a91/f97/8700dd31637391b5626d0ddf3a9095f8d1-2026-Scary-Movie-6.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

What convinced you to come back for Scary Movie 6 after taking a break from the Brenda character?It was definitely the return of the Wayanses and the original cast. I love Anna, and I love our characters Brenda and Cindy — I’ve played Brenda more than any character ever.

Harvey Weinstein famously lowballed the Wayanses after the success of Scary Movie and Scary Movie 2. They walked away from the franchise, and Miramax made Scary Movies 3, 4, and 5 without them. In Scary Movie 6, they address this head-on, even asking Brenda why she made the sequels without them. Did you have any personal conversations about that? There was a warm and meaningful conversation had many years ago. I actually applauded that they addressed it in a humorous way. Obviously, Marlon had talked about it in interviews. I think that at the point of the first and second movies, you’re treated as an actor for hire. I thought the way they wrote that into the script was clever.

Did you have any concern about being typecast or seen as doing only “Black comedy”?I didn’t think about it in terms of Black, just more about characters — like, Okay, so this is Brenda. So maybe I don’t need to do another version of that movie. But I think there’s no way not to be aware of that. I want to sound lofty and say I made a decision, but it was only my third film and, at the end of the day, you still have to work. I was afforded enough work to say, I won’t do that one again and again. So I think the only role I’ve ever done that is that extreme and that broad is Brenda. But, I mean, I’ve done her now five times.

How did you navigate that increase in visibility?I still had friends who were five times more famous, so it wasn’t a big thing. Gabrielle Union was killing it at the time, Sanaa Lathan was bigger from Love & Basketball, I think Nia Long was doing Big Momma’s House. I was one of many. You’re still grinding and working and auditioning; it’s just people recognize you. You’re still trying to get to the thing that you think is going to be the thing — I don’t even know if that thing is real. I just really always felt blessed to work. I had this incredible group of young, aspiring, and inspiring women, and we came up at a time when it was really fun and everybody was very supportive. There was no social media.

There are certainly perks — you can get a couple things free, and people are nice. But then there’d be a lot of places where I wasn’t recognized or where people would be like, “I’m such a big fan. I love you, Miss King.”
I always forget that you and Regina King get mixed up.All the time.

Were there roles you really wanted that you didn’t get?For some reason, the one that sticks out was in 2001’s Josie and the Pussycats. It was down to the wire, the last four or five, and Left Eye was there. I don’t remember what she wore, but whatever she wore to the audition, I liked. I don’t know why I was so upset when I didn’t get it. Rosario Dawson got it.
And then, of course, there are other roles I went and auditioned for and didn’t get, but then I loved who got it. I love Taraji P. Henson in Baby Boy — I auditioned for that, but she was amazing.

After Scary Movie 2 in 2001, you were in Malibu’s Most Wanted and Death at a Funeral. And then you have Think Like a Man, which was an unexpected hit with a lot of longtime collaborators, followed by The Best Man Holiday. How was it working on those films?Think Like a Man was just fun. I knew Michael Ealy when we were auditioning in New York. Kevin Hart and I had done Scary Movie 3 and 4, and he was also in Death at a Funeral. Taraji and I were friends. I did not know Meagan Good well, but Meagan used to go to a church that I went to, and so she’s always been very devoted and spiritual. It was really fun to play with Taraji; it’s great when you get to work with someone who you respect and like.

Best Man Holiday was like a Y’all, I can’t believe we’re doing this together 15 years later. So that was like a family reunion of sorts, and I had no expectations on how either would do. I try not to think like that. You’ll be like, “Oh, it’s gonna do this,” and then it doesn’t. But I do remember that I read the scripts for both, and they were both really good. Those sets felt like a home-cooked meal. There is something incredibly rich about doing a “Black comedy” as well. You’re going to work, but you’re also playing. Those two movies felt special.

Let’s talk about Girls Trip. That seemed like a watershed cultural moment for Black women in comedy.I certainly felt it was special. But I felt it was special because we liked one another and I had never met them; I didn’t know them beyond a “hi and bye.” When we did our first sit-down rehearsal, I knew it was going to be special. I remember liking each of those women onscreen for different reasons, then I remember the studio was getting excited about it and I was like, “Y’all getting too excited.” But it ended up being a great surprise. And I was so thrilled by how people responded to that film.

What made the film resonate, at least in my circles, was how relatable it felt.It’s funny because I was telling Malcolm when we were doing rewrites of the draft, you know, when women are around each other, we don’t talk that sweet. We’re not just sipping wine on vacation. I thought those moments were authentic enough to show us as smart and vulnerable and ratchet and loving and classy and all the things we are when we’re in safe spaces with our closest people.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/d1c/b68/a42be94f84b23ed2d009fd8ed162f59176-2017-Girls-Trip.rhorizontal.w700.jpg
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/e2c/b89/6b4e548157ed078b85cb7e3129dc47a7b9-2018-Support-the-Girls.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

I know you say you don’t pay attention to whether a movie is successful at the box office. But do you pay attention to critical feedback?It depends. I mean, a film like Girls Trip is very connected to audiences’ opinions and the financial success of the movie, while I feel like Support the Girls was very connected to critics and they were the reason it became what it was.

I’m glad you brought up Support the Girls, actually, because it seems there was a distinct shift in how critics wrote about you after the success of that film. They suddenly saw you as a dramatic actor with interiority.Yeah, that’s true. I think it was the timing of Support the Girls and Girls Trip because they came out very close to each other. And in Girls Trip, although it was a comedy, I was the straighter one of the characters. With Support the Girls, the critics were incredibly generous and kind and supportive, and I think it broadened our audience, including a critical audience. We won the New York Film Critics Circle Award; it was wonderful.

There was also a shift in the opportunities I had, as in how people perceived not just the stuff that came after but maybe the sum total of my career because there was enough work to look back on. That definitely made a big difference.

Do you feel comedic actors have to prove their depth? Or is that an overstated perception?I never really looked at it in that way. I have friends who are incredible dramatic actresses who want to do comedy. You’re an artist, so you always want to be able to live and experience and show as many colors as you can. From an industry point of view, you can simply look at it and say, “Well, gosh, it’s interesting that there’s not a category in the Oscars for comedy.” That does exist at the Golden Globes, and it’s just really that one.
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/4a5/3f7/f9b7536923d7f8e6ba02b9c5472d50d71e-2022-Honk-for-Jesus-Save-Your-Soul.rhorizontal.w700.jpg
https://pyxis.nymag.com/v1/imgs/ca6/30e/d4a7d90944015cd22825b8f2fd057016ba-2025-One-Battle-After-Another.rhorizontal.w700.jpg

Let’s talk about your performance in Honk for Jesus. I think it’s a brilliant satire of the Black church. You’re also playing a character who’s so obsessed with how she is seen and received.I went and talked to and researched so many First Ladies. I never thought about the position of First Lady in a church to that degree and the weight and the responsibility they felt. So I think in terms of her dutifulness, I definitely connected to the pride of that, though she went far beyond what I would probably do.

I want to talk a little bit about One Battle After Another. What was it like to go on such an aggressive press tour for a film that was clearly gunning for awards?Portions of it were really exhausting. I will say, thankfully, at least, we all got along and liked one another. I had the support of the girls with Teyana Taylor and Chase Infiniti, and they had a lot more of the heavy lifting because they were nominated for so many things. They had to do far more than I had to do.

There was speculation about you getting nominated for Best Supporting Actress for OBAA. Is that a level of recognition in this industry that you are still striving for?I don’t know that I necessarily spend a lot of time pondering that. In the moment, I always want to do great work; that’s really my goal. If an award comes out of that, then that’s great. You want to do award-worthy work, but my head doesn’t live in that space. I feel really happy, and I love when I’m working. I also love when I’m not working too — by choice, I should say [laughs].

We last see your character in a van. Do you think she survives that, or do you think she’s in prison?You know what I thought? She got killed. Paul’s take is that she’s in prison. I think they took it to the side and took her out.

I want to know what Paul Thomas Anderson is like as a neighbor.Great, and good movie collection. He’s a great guy with a great family. His partner, Maya Rudolph, is great as well. They’re more normal than you’d think.

You participated in a Kevin Hart roast on Netflix in May. How did you feel doing it? Were you involved in writing your part?I’m not touching the roast right now; that’s just too hot. But I always throw a little hand in writing it. I always feel it makes it a little more personal when you add a bit of your own touch. Especially for something that’s personal, and especially since I’m not even — though people get confused — I’m not a stand-up. I was very impressed with the ladies that night.

You have played so many dynamic Black women over the years. Do you think the industry has meaningfully evolved in how Black women are written and cast?There’s always room for growth and improvement. If it hasn’t happened in the real world, I’m sure it isn’t fully complete in art.
